questions.question: Explain the purpose of 'this' pointer in C++ with an example.
keyPoints: 'this' is an implicit pointer pointing to current object
Available in all non-static member functions
Used to distinguish member variables from parameters with same name
Example: this->x = x; or (*this).x = x;

questions.question: Differentiate between new and delete operators in C++ with respect to object creation and destruction.
keyPoints: new allocates memory from heap for objects
new calls constructor automatically
delete calls destructor then deallocates memory
Always use delete for memory allocated by new to prevent memory leak

questions.question: What is the arrow operator (->) in C++? How is it used with object pointers?
keyPoints: Arrow operator accesses members through pointers
Syntax: pointer->member_name
Equivalent to (*pointer).member_name
More readable than dereferencing then using dot operator

questions.question: Explain upcasting and downcasting with respect to class pointers.
keyPoints: Upcasting: Derived class pointer/address assigned to base class pointer
Upcasting is always safe (implicit conversion)
Downcasting: Base class pointer assigned to derived class pointer
Downcasting requires explicit cast and is unsafe

questions.question: Why is virtual destructor necessary when using polymorphism with object pointers?
keyPoints: Virtual destructor ensures proper cleanup order
When delete called on base pointer pointing to derived object
Derived class destructor executes before base destructor
Prevents undefined behavior and memory leaks

questions.question: Write a C++ program to demonstrate dynamic object creation using new and delete operators.
keyPoints: Use class with constructor and destructor
Create object: ClassName *ptr = new ClassName(args);
Access members using ptr->member
Delete object: delete ptr; to call destructor

questions.question: What are the advantages and disadvantages of using pointers to objects in C++?
keyPoints: Advantages: Dynamic memory allocation, polymorphism, efficient passing to functions
Disadvantages: Memory leaks, dangling pointers, complexity, no bounds checking

questions.question: Explain the concept of polymorphism using pointers to objects. Illustrate with a program showing base class pointer pointing to derived class objects.
keyPoints: Define base class with virtual function
Define derived classes overriding virtual function
Create derived class objects dynamically
Use base class pointer to call derived class methods
Demonstrate runtime polymorphism through virtual functions

questions.question: Write a complete C++ program to implement a class representing a Bank Account with dynamic memory allocation for account holder name. Include constructors, destructor, and member functions to display account details using object pointers.
keyPoints: Class with dynamic data member (char* or string for name)
Parameterized constructor for initialization
Destructor to deallocate dynamic memory
Display function using this pointer or object pointer
Create objects statically and dynamically, demonstrate access

questions.question: Explain the difference between static and dynamic object creation in C++. Write a program to demonstrate that destructors are called automatically for both cases.
keyPoints: Static: allocated on stack, automatic cleanup when out of scope
Dynamic: allocated on heap using new, manual cleanup with delete required
Show constructor and destructor calls for both
Demonstrate memory leak if delete not used for dynamic objects

questions.question: What is a dangling pointer? How can it be avoided? Explain with examples in context of object pointers.
keyPoints: Dangling pointer points to deallocated or invalid memory
Causes: deleting memory then using pointer, pointer to local variable
Solutions: set pointer to nullptr after delete, use smart pointers
Example showing dangling pointer problem and solution
